---
title: "Bristly Greenbrier (Smilax tamnoides)"
slug: "smilax-tamnoides"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474875284/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Bristly Greenbrier"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Liliales"
tax_family: "Smilacaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Liliales", "Smilacaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474875284/medium.jpg" alt="Bristly Greenbrier" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/ahevesh">
ahevesh</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264445140">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Liliales
-   **Family:** Smilacaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=128943)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in forests, thickets, and fencerows.

## Natural history notes

A climbing vine characterized by its woody, thorny stems and broad, heart-shaped leaves. In Florida, it is found in a variety of habitats including forests, thickets, and fencerows, where it climbs over surrounding vegetation. It grows during the warmer months and produces clusters of dark berries that are eaten by wildlife.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/128943-Smilax-tamnoides)
-   [Local profile](https://explorer.natureserve.org/Taxon/ELEMENT_GLOBAL.2.155658/Smilax_tamnoides)
